paper_title: SoundWeaver: Semantic Warm-Starting for Text-to-Audio Diffusion Serving

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Text-to-audio diffusion models produce high-fidelity audio but require tens of function evaluations (NFEs), incurring multi-second latency and limited throughput. We present SoundWeaver, the first training-free, model-agnostic serving system that accelerates text-to-audio diffusion by warm-starting from semantically similar cached audio. SoundWeaver introduces three components: a Reference Selector that retrieves and temporally aligns cached candidates via semantic and duration-aware gating; a Skip Gater that dynamically determines the percentage of NFEs to skip; and a lightweight Cache Manager that maintains cache utility through quality-aware eviction and refinement. On real-world audio traces, SoundWeaver achieves 1.8--3.0$ \times $ latency reduction with a cache of only ${\sim}$1K entries while preserving or improving perceptual quality.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: SoundWeaver: Semantic Warm-Starting for Text-to-Audio Diffusion Serving Ayush Barik ID 1, Sofia Stoica ID 1, Nikhil Sarda ID 2, Arnav Kethana ID 1, Abhinav Khanduja ID 1, Muchen Xu ID 1, Fan Lai ID 1 1 University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, USA 2 Assured Intelligence, USA barik2@illinois.edu, sstoica2@illinois.edu, nikhilsarda.iitkgp@gmail.com, kethana2@illinois.edu, AK89@illinois.edu, muchenx2@illinois.edu, fanlai@illinois.edu 6 2 0 2 ] . [ 1 5 6 8 7 0 . 3 0 6 2 : r Abstract Text-to-audio diffusion models produce high-fidelity audio but require tens of function evaluations (NFEs), incurring multi-second latency and limited throughput. We present SoundWeaver, the first training-free, model-agnostic serving system that accelerates text-to-audio diffusion by warm-starting from semantically similar cached audio. SoundWeaver introduces three components: Reference Selector that retrieves and temporally aligns cached candidates via semantic and durationaware gating; Skip Gater that dynamically determines the percentage of NFEs to skip; and lightweight Cache Manager that maintains cache utility through quality-aware eviction and refinement. On real-world audio traces, SoundWeaver achieves 1.83.0 latency reduction with cache of only 1K entries while preserving or improving perceptual quality. Index Terms: Text-to-Audio, Retrieval-Augmented Generation, Diffusion, Systems for Audio 1. Introduction Diffusion models have rapidly become the foundation of textto-audio (T2A) generation for applications such as music composition and sound-effect synthesis [1, 2]. These models iteratively denoise Gaussian noise into high-fidelity waveforms or spectrograms over tens of steps while conditioning on natural language prompts. Despite their quality advantages, inference remains inherently computationally intensive: generating audio clips can require multiple seconds on modern GPUs [3]. At production scale, where services process millions of requests daily [4], this translates into high user-perceived latency and substantial infrastructure cost. To mitigate this efficiency bottleneck, prior algorithmic advances have primarily focused on reducing the number of function evaluations (NFE). Algorithmic advances include improved samplers [5, 6], adaptive timestep scheduling [7], early-exit strategies [8], and distillation that compresses multi-step diffusion into few-step samplers [9, 10]. Concurrently, systemsoriented efforts have targeted multi-GPU parallelism, and hardwaresoftware co-design [11, 12]. We explore complementary, underexamined opportunity: exploiting inherent semantic similarity in audio distributions to reduce NFEs. As shown in Figure 1, analysis of realistic traces (e.g., AudioCaps [13]) reveals that most user-uploaded audios have close semantic neighbors. In diffusion models, early NFEs establish coarse, low-frequency structure, while later steps refine high-frequency perceptual detail [14, 15]. Semantically similar samples, therefore, share structural components, allowing cached neighbor to act as strong priorwarm-starting from an intermediate step [16] and skipping the NFEs that construct coarse structure. Figure 1: Distribution of CLAP scores for nearest-neighbor retrievals across AudioCaps prompts. Building on this insight, we present SoundWeaver, which works through audio-guided warm-starting, which involves maintaining reference cache and, upon new request, selecting semantically aligned candidate to warm-start generation from an intermediate state, skipping initial NFEs. Enabling such approximate caching introduces non-trivial challenges, which we address with the following contributions: novel Reference Selector that performs semantic and duration-aware retrieval and alignment from the cache (2.1). Skip Gater that balances qualitylatency trade-offs and adapts to the user-prompt distribution to determine the percentage of NFEs to skip during diffusion (2.2). lightweight Cache Manager that performs quality-aware eviction and refinement, ensuring high reuse utility while bounding memory and compute overhead. (2.3). By transforming audio similarity into computation savings, SoundWeaver substantially improves serving latency. Our evaluations on realistic workloads show that SoundWeaver achieves 1.83.0 end-to-end latency speedup, while preservingand often even improvingperceptual quality with cache of only 1K audio entries (3). 2. Methods T2A diffusion models learn reverse process pθ(xt1xt, c) that iteratively denoises latent from pure noise xT (0, I) toward clean audio latent x0 over steps, conditioned on text prompt c. SoundWeaver warm-starts this reverse process using semantically aligned cached reference ˆx0. Rather than initializing from pure noise, we sample an intermediate latent via the forward process q(xt ˆx0) at skip timestep < , and run the reverse process from xt , reducing the required NFEs from to t. The semantic structure of ˆx0 acts as prior steering generation toward c, with greater semantic alignment permitting more aggressive skipping. However, realizing consistent gains requires addressing gaps of prior works such as duration alignment between ˆx0 and the target-request duration, preserving output diversity, and adaptively selecting t. audio supports continuous time-stretching that can adjust duration while largely preserving perceptual content. We therefore extend the quality gate to incorporate relaxed duration compatibility by redefining qi as: Overview. SoundWeaver consists of three key components, which will be defined in the following sections: 1 the Reference Selector (2.1), 2 the Skip Gater (2.2), and 3 the Cache Manager (2.3). Given text prompt, the Reference Selector retrieves top-K semantically similar audio samples from the cache, filters them using quality gate, and stochastically selects candidate before running it through phase vocoder which temporally aligns the candidate to the requested duration. The aligned audio is passed to the Skip Gater, which determines the optimal skip percentage to warm-start diffusion at an intermediate step. Meanwhile, the Cache Manager asynchronously maintains the cache while ensuring runtime quality. 2.1. Reference Selector Given user prompt, the Reference Selector identifies cached audio that maximizes NFE skipping while preserving generation quality. This decision requires jointly considering (i) semantic alignment with the new request (ii) output diversity and (iii) length compatibility with the requested duration. Quality-Aware Reference Retrieval. SoundWeaver introduces gating mechanism that enforces quality and duration constraints on top-K retrieved candidates while preserving diversity. For each candidate i, we compute two CLAP scores: spos(i), the CLAP score between the candidates audio embedding and the user prompt, and sneg(i), the similarity to fixed negative prompt (e.g., low quality). Candidates are sampled proportionally to similarity: pi exp(spos(i)/τ ), where τ controls diversity. To prevent degradation from weak candidates, we apply quality gate admitting only samples satisfying qi θq, where: (cid:20) qi = min spos(i) maxj spos(j) , 1 sneg(i) maxj (1 sneg(j)) (cid:21) (1) We refer to the first term as ai (normalized positive similarity) and the second as bi (normalized negative dissimilarity) on the right side of the equation. θq is predefined quality threshold value. For scalability, candidate embeddings are indexed using FAISS [17], which applies coarse K-means clustering followed by hierarchical approximate nearest-neighbor search. To further improve cache coverage, we introduce pyramid indexing scheme that materializes CLAP embeddings at multiple temporal granularities down to configurable minimum granularity δ (e.g., 1 4 of the clip duration). Concretely, long audio clip is partitioned into multi-scale segments, each associated with its own embedding, enabling retrieval to match the most semantically aligned portion of cached audio rather than the entire clip. Importantly, this design does not increase audio storage overhead, as we retain only single full-resolution copy of each audio and index lightweight segment-level embeddings. Duration-Aware Adaptation. Durations of T2A generation requests can vary substantially, and diffusion latents are inherently duration-dependent, preventing direct reuse of cached clips with mismatched lengths. Unlike text or image generation, (cid:40) qi = min(ai, bi) 0 if di [0.5L, 1.5L] otherwise (2) where di is the duration of candidate and is the requested audio duration. Strict duration matching (di = L) would significantly limit cache utility. Instead, we admit candidates within compatible range and delegate precise alignment to lightweight phase vocoder [18], which performs frequencydomain time-scaling while preserving pitch. Unlike timedomain methods such as WSOLA [19], suited primarily to monophonic signals, the phase vocoder operates on full STFT representations and better handles the polyphonic soundscapes typical of T2A workloads [13, 20]. Although phase vocoders can introduce artifacts (e.g., phasiness, transient smearing) at extreme stretch ratios [21], our duration gate restricts candidates to modest factors where such effects remain negligible (3.1). 2.2. Skip Gater Even with cache candidate for warm-starting, deciding how many NFEs to skip (e.g., 10060) is crucial to balancing efficiency and quality. Rule-based heuristics (e.g., fixed skip thresholds based on similarity) fail to generalize across prompts and model behaviors, as optimal skip ratios depend on request semantics and intrinsic generation difficulty. Training supervised predictor is also impractical due to evolving request distributions and the lack of golden labels for quality-aware skipping. We introduce contextual multi-arm bandit (MAB)-based controller to explore and exploit the best skip percentage online, where each arm corresponds to predefined skip percentage ({0%, 5%, 10%, . . . , 65%}). Given context features {eprompt, ecache, }, where eprompt and ecache are text/audio embeddings provided by the Reference Selector and denotes the total NFEs, the bandit selects an arm that determines the percentage of total NFEs to skip. The reward for updating MABs policy can be defined as: rt = α Et + (1 α) Qt (3) where α (0, 1) denotes the trade-off between efficiency gain and generation quality; Et denotes normalized efficiency gain (e.g., number of NFEs skipped); and Qt denotes the perceptual quality metric (e.g., CLAP score). We optimized α using bayesian optimization [22] over α [0.01, 0.5], yielding an optimal value of α = 0.47. Rank-based reward normalization. In practice, absolute quality scores are often noisy: users rarely provide reliable scalar ratings, and automatic proxies such as CLAP similarity may not perfectly capture perceptual quality. By contrast, production systems naturally collect preference-style signals (e.g., thumbs up/down or pairwise comparisons). We can leverage such ranking feedback to avoid additional data collection overhead, by converting Qt into relative rank-based score: Qt = rank(Qt) Np 1 [0, 1] (4) where Np is the number of available arms (i.e., skip choices). This normalization transforms absolute quality differences into stable, more robust relative ordering. Figure 2: SoundWeaver overview and request execution flow. Prompt-variance-weighted training. Skip decisions affect prompts unevenly. Our analysis of 987 randomly selected examples from the AudioCaps training dataset shows that CLAP variance spans nearly two orders of magnitude: for simple prompts (e.g., Water runs continuously), perceptual quality remains stable across skip ratios, while for other more semantically rich prompts (e.g., Someone types really fast on an old typewriter and the handle rings...) small skip changes cause noticeable degradation. To learn semantically-aware MAB offline, let σ2 denote the observed variance of quality across skip options for prompt p. We weight training updates by wp = σ2 σ2 + ϵ (5) where σ2 is the mean variance across prompts and ϵ is small constant for numerical stability. Intuitively, prompts where skip choices meaningfully impact quality (high σ2 p) contribute more to learning, while skip-insensitive prompts are downweighted. Conditioned on this weighting, the model learns two things simultaneously: how good each skip decision is expected to be (learned by minimizing E[wp δ2 ]), and how confident it should be in that estimate (learned by minimizing E[wp (ˆu(st, at) δt)2]): = E[wp δ2 ] + λ E[wp (ˆu(st, at) δt)2] (6) where st is the state, at is the action, and rt is the reward at timestep t, respectively; ˆu(st, at) is the predicted uncertainty; and δt = ˆQ(st, at) rt is the TD-error, and λ is hyperparameter. Preliminary results showed λ = 0.1 worked well. We train MAB offline during off-peak hours to not interfere with request execution and completion. 2.3. Cache Manager At runtime, the Cache Manager monitors the utility of each cache, evicting stale, low-scoring entries and inserting newly generated user audio. Additionally, it selectively replays and refines frequently retrieved but low-quality entries to maximize re-usability for future requests. Cache Eviction. When the cache reaches capacity, the Cache Manager evicts entries with the lowest importance score. For cache entry i, we define its importance as apply exponential decay Ii Iiγt where γ = 0.9 (per hour) and is the elapsed time in hours since the last update. This mechanism prioritizes recently beneficial entries while gradually discounting stale ones. Lightweight Cache Refinement. To improve long-term cache quality, the Cache Manager selectively refines frequently reused but low-quality entries during idle periods. When cached audio produces poor results (e.g, too few NFE skips), the system chooses the best out of three regenerations. Our evaluations show that such replay improves CLAP score by 0.27 evaluated across 975 prompts from AudioCaps [13]. We limit each prompt to at most five regeneration attempts to ensure that all cached prompts have fair opportunity for refinement and not one prompt dominates. 3. Evaluation Experimental Setup. All experiments are conducted on an A100 GPU. We build the cache from Clotho v2 [23], which contains 1,045 audio clips of diverse durations. Following existing works [1, 2], we evaluate on AudioCaps, randomly sampling one caption per clip as the generation prompt. Consistent with retrieval-augmented generation studies [24, 25], we adopt AudioLDM 1 (652M) as the primary backbone and additionally evaluate AudioLDM 2 2 (1.1B) for completeness. Both models are ran with 200 NFEs using the DDIM sampler. Baseline and Metrics. We take vanilla generation without caching or NFE skipping as our baseline. We report four (1) CLAP Score [26], measurstandard objective metrics: ing semantic alignment between audio generations and their prompts; (2) KL Divergence [27], quantifying instance-level divergence in acoustic event posteriors; (3) Frechet Distance (FD) [28], capturing distributional similarity; and (4) Inception Score (IS) [29], evaluating both fidelity and diversity. Finally, we conduct LLM-as-a-judge evaluation using Gemini-3-Flash [30], observing its success in speech [31]. Following the LMArena pairwise framework [32], we compute Preference Score (PS) based on pairwise win rates between SoundWeaver and baseline outputs under identical prompts, reflecting subjective perceptual quality. Ii = (cid:88) tUi (St Dt) (7) 3.1. Main Results where Ui denotes the set of reuse events for entry i, St is the number of skipped NFEs for request t, and Dt is the audio duration. This score captures the total generation savings contributed by this entry. To adapt to evolving request patterns, we We evaluate SoundWeaver using two cache variants: (1) realaudio cache constructed from the original recordings corre1https://github.com/haoheliu/AudioLDM 2https://github.com/haoheliu/AudioLDM2 synthetic-audio Table 1: Generation quality and latency. cache; real-audio cache. Ablations remove the Reference Selector (w/o RS) and Skip Gater (w/o SG). Table 2: Ablation of cache size on FSD50K. Larger caches yield consistently better generation quality. Model Method FD KL IS CLAP PS Latency Audio LDM 39.78 2.04 5.15 Baseline SW 34.81 2.05 5.10 SW (w/o RS) 32.34 1.93 5.52 SW (w/o SG) 29.37 1.82 5.49 SW 28.83 1.83 5.71 Audio LDM2 Baseline SW SW 25.97 1.88 7.41 31.15 2.10 6.24 26.92 1.88 7.24 0.32 0.33 0.35 0.35 0.36 0.29 0.31 0.32 0.48 0.57 7.93s 4.50s 4.42s 4.36s 4.37s 14.85s 6.21s 0.43 0.60 6.59s (a) AudioLDM model. (b) AudioLDM2 model. Figure 3: Serving latency in online deployments. SW leverages synthetic-audio cache; SW uses real-audio cache. sponding to the cache prompts, and (2) synthetic cache consisting of model-generated audio for those same prompts. SoundWeaver improves generation latency and quality. Table 1 shows that SoundWeaver achieves 1.81 and 2.25 latency speedup for AudioLDM and AudioLDM2, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of audio-guided warm-starting. For AudioLDM, both cache variants improve over the baseline across all quality metrics with the real-audio cache yielding the largest gains. We attribute this improvement to the richer perceptual information in real recordings, consistent with prior findings that real audio improves generation quality [24, 25]. The comparable IS across all conditions further indicates that SoundWeaver does not compromise output diversity. For AudioLDM2, both variants introduce slight degradation relative to the baseline, though the real-audio cache outperforms the synthetic cache and both remain competitive while delivering substantial latency reductions. These results are achieved with cache of only 1K entries. SoundWeaver improves performance in online deployments. Figure 3 shows per-request latency over one-hour online deployment for AudioLDM and AudioLDM2. We use realistic request traces derived from DiffusionDB [33], with arrival rates scaled to our hardware capacity. Across both backbones, SoundWeaver consistently reduces serving latency by warm-starting from cached audio, significantly lowering the number of NFEs per request. This yields 2.7 and 3.0 endto-end latency speedups for AudioLDM and AudioLDM2, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of SoundWeaver in

sections.title: Cache Size
sections.content: FD KL IS CLAP 100 500 1000 2000 5000 51.92 46.72 42.40 33.60 31.62 2.99 2.63 2.54 1.93 1. 5.17 5.58 5.94 5.87 6.22 0.19 0.23 0.24 0.32 0.33 real-world deployment scenarios. 3.2. Ablation Studies Performance Breakdown. Table 1 presents an ablation study of SoundWeavers components. For the Reference Selector ablation (SW w/o RS), we remove pyramid indexing, the quality gate, and stochastic top-k sampling, replacing them with nearest-neighbor retrieval strategy similar to prior image generation work [34]. The phase vocoder is retained for duration alignment. The resulting quality degradation indicates that these components jointly improve retrieval effectiveness and downstream generation quality. For the Skip Gater ablation (SW w/o SG), we replace our adaptive gating mechanism with rule-based skip policy used in prior image generation systems [16, 34], which applies stepwise skipping based on similarity thresholds (e.g., skipping 55% of denoising steps when similarity 0.35). For fair comparison, we align our Skip Gater to comparable latency point, demonstrating that it provides similar quality benefits, whilst being dynamic solution to shifting SLOs and workloads. Impact of Cache Pool Size. We evaluate SoundWeaver under varying cache pool sizes by sampling different numbers of audio clips from FSD50K [35] to construct caches of increasing size. Table 2 reports generation quality while holding latency constant. As expected, larger caches improve quality, since the Reference Selector retrieves more semantically aligned starting points. Notably, SoundWeaver matches or exceeds fulldenoising quality once the cache reaches approximately 2K entries, reaffirming its effectiveness even with small cache. SoundWeaver introduces minimal overhead. The Reference Selector and Skip Gater add an average 0.04s overhead per request. Meanwhile, the Cache Manager runs asynchronously during off-peak hours, avoiding serving latency impacts; each refined entry is reused hundreds of times, heavily amortizing costs across requests to yield 1% overhead. 4. Conclusion We introduce SoundWeaver, fundamentally new, modelagnostic approach to serving T2A diffusion models. By leveraging audio-guided warm-starting, SoundWeaver speeds up generation by 1.8 to 3.0 while maintaining or improving perceptual quality, adding minimal overhead in the process. While promising, limitations still remain which include phase vocoder distortion on long audio requests, lack of dedicated request-schedulers, and untested compatibility with complex samplers, which inspire further future work. 5. Acknowledgments The authors would like to thank ISCA and the organizing committees of past Interspeech conferences for their help and for kindly providing the previous version of this template. 6. Generative AI Use Disclosure We use generative AI for two purposes. First, we leverage it to refine the writing and presentation of the paper. Second, following current practice using LLM-as-a-judge [32], we employ Gemini 3 Flash as an automated evaluator to compute preference scores for generated outputs. The evaluation prompt used for this evaluation is provided below. Gemini Audio Evaluation Prompt You are an expert audio quality evaluator. You will be given two audio clips generated from the same text prompt and need to choose the audio file that sounds better. Your evaluation should consider consistency, intelligibility, naturalness, absence of artifacts, timbral richness, fidelity, and overall quality. Avoid any position biases and ensure that the order in which the audio were presented does not influence your decision. Do not allow the duration of the audio to influence your evaluation. Be as objective as possible. You should start with your evaluation by comparing the two audios and provide short rationale about which one you think is better. After providing your rationale, you should output the final verdict by strictly following this seven-point Likert scale: Compare the two audio clips and score the difference on scale from -3 to 3:

sections.title: Score Meaning
sections.content: +3 +2 +1 0 1 2 3 Audio 1 is much better than Audio 2 Audio 1 is noticeably better Audio 1 is slightly better Both are equal / indistinguishable Audio 2 is slightly better Audio 2 is noticeably better Audio 2 is much better than Audio 1 Respond strictly in this format: [Rationale]: short rationale, less than 200 words [Score]: integer from 3 to +3 7.
